description: Official QuaRot (Int4+FP16-had) vs CDA (K4V2+HMMA) kernel latency, decode attention only.
shape_note: QuaRot kernel is MHA-only (no GQA). We bench both at their native shapes.
quarot_shape: H_q=32 H_kv=32 D=128 (Llama-2-7B MHA). Int4 K + Int4 V with per-token asymmetric scale/zero, fp16 Hadamard rotation.
cda_shape: H_q=32 H_kv=8 D=128 (Llama-3.1-8B GQA, group_size=4). K4V2 codebook + fp32 norms.
cda_impl: torch.ops._C.cda_decode_full_hmma (register-fragment synthesis, no fp16 K/V materialization)

kv_memory_per_token.quarot_int4_mha_32heads: 32 heads * (4-bit K + 4-bit V) * 128 D + 4*(fp16 scale+zero K,V) = 1024 B/token
kv_memory_per_token.cda_k4v2_gqa_8heads: 8 heads * (4-bit K + 2-bit V) * 128 D + 8*(fp32 norm K,V) = 104 B/token
kv_memory_per_token.ratio: CDA reads 9.85x less KV per token vs QuaRot Int4

caveats: Shape mismatch: QuaRot MHA has 4x more KV heads than CDA GQA — not apples-to-apples in compute, but faithfully reflects what each method's released kernel can serve.
Modern Llama-3/Mistral/Qwen use GQA. QuaRot would need kernel re-engineering to match; CDA ships GQA natively.
Both kernels exclude softmax numerics differences — same Q/K/V random data at each shape.

summary.B_1_range: CDA 3.77-9.16× faster than QuaRot across N=4K-64K
summary.headline: CDA 1.97-9.16× faster than QuaRot's official kernel across the full (B, N) grid.
summary.key_narrative: QuaRot's Int4 is actually SLOWER than its own FP16 baseline at B=1 (decompress-then-attend overhead). CDA avoids this because attention is computed directly on compressed indices.
